coredata.dc:title: Rapid and selective detection of nitrite in ham sausage and water samples by a portable gas pressure meter
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Jing
Tang, Jiayuan
Chen, Jihong
Li, Lanlan
Zhang, Shu
Xiong, Xiaoli
Zou, Zhirong
coredata.dc:description: A facile and portable method was established for the detection of nitrite (NO2 -) by a portable gas pressure meter. NO2 - and sodium cyclamate were reacted specifically under acidic condition, cyclohexene and nitrogen were thus generated, the pressures of reaction bottles were measured by a portable gas pressure meter. The pressures in bottles were increased gradually and proportional to the increase of NO2 - concentration, the detection of NO2 - was thus achieved. This method ensured good selectivity for the detection of NO2 - and avoided interferences from other coexisting ions. In the range of 2–100 mg L−1 (43–2174 μmol L−1), the gas pressure was linearly correlated with the NO2 - concentration, and a limit of detection of 0.65 mg L−1 (14 μmol L−1) was obtained. The developed method was further used for NO2 - detection in water and ham sausage samples, recoveries of 94–106 % were obtained. It is a potential simple, rapid, portable analytical system/method for on-site detection of NO2 -.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite (NO2 -)
Gas pressure meter
Sodium cyclamate
Portable
Ham sausage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The feasibility of the gas generation reaction between NO2 - and sodium cyclamate.
The optimized conditions for NO2 - (6 mL, 50 mg L−1). (a) Concentration of sodium cyclamate, (b) concentration of H2SO4, (c) reaction temperature and (d) reaction time.
The stability for NO2 - detection by gas pressure meter (concentration of NO2 -: 50 mg L−1). The total number of bottles is 20.
Interferences assessment of the proposed method. Concentration of NO2 -: 50 mg L−1, concentration of co-existing ions: 500 mg L−1.
Linear relationship of gas pressure meter (a) and Griess method (b) with different concentration of NO2 -.
The schematic diagram of NO2 - detection by portable gas pressure meter.
Analytical figures of merit of this work for NO2 - detection compared with other methods.
Analytical results of NO2 - in water samples and ham sausage samples by gas pressure meter and Griess method.

head.ce:title: Rapid and selective detection of nitrite in ham sausage and water samples by a portable gas pressure meter

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources (Ministry of Education), Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources (Ministry of Education), Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources (Ministry of Education), Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610068, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and portable method was established for the detection of nitrite (NO2 -) by a portable gas pressure meter. NO2 - and sodium cyclamate were reacted specifically under acidic condition, cyclohexene and nitrogen were thus generated, the pressures of reaction bottles were measured by a portable gas pressure meter. The pressures in bottles were increased gradually and proportional to the increase of NO2 - concentration, the detection of NO2 - was thus achieved. This method ensured good selectivity for the detection of NO2 - and avoided interferences from other coexisting ions. In the range of 2–100 mg L−1 (43–2174 μmol L−1), the gas pressure was linearly correlated with the NO2 - concentration, and a limit of detection of 0.65 mg L−1 (14 μmol L−1) was obtained. The developed method was further used for NO2 - detection in water and ham sausage samples, recoveries of 94–106 % were obtained. It is a potential simple, rapid, portable analytical system/method for on-site detection of NO2 -.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 -) is a potential carcinogen that harmful to humans [1], and is widely utilized as an industrial salt in foods, such as coloring, preservation, antibacterial agent and additive [2,3]. Excessive intake of NO2 - can cause a series of diseases. NO2 - can interfere the body's oxygen transport system, causing the irreversible conversion of hemoglobin to methemoglobin, the ability to exchange oxygen of hemoglobin can be impaired severely [4–6]. NO2 - also can undergo reactions with secondary amines and amides in the stomach, leading to the formation of carcinogenic N-nitrosamines[7,8]. This process was associated with a range of medical conditions, including cancer, hypertension, and neurodegenerative disorder [1]. In addition, the excessive use of nitrogen fertilizer also lead to NO2 - pollution in soil and environmental water system [9]. Required by the World Health Organization (WHO), the allowable concentration level of NO2 - in water does not exceed 3 mg L−1 [10,11], and the acceptable daily amount of NO2 - in food is 0.06 mg kg−1 [12]. NO2 - is an important index of food safety and water quality [13]. Therefore, the monitor of NO2 - levels in food and water accurately is of great significance.

Nowadays, various methods have been reported for the detection of NO2 -, including spectrophotometry [14,15], electrochemical methods [16,17], capillary electrophoresis [18,19], ion chromatography [20,21], fluorescence spectrometry [22–24] and so on. However, the use of these methods were always limited in the laboratory, bulky instruments and professional operators were required, it is not conducive to on-site real-time analysis. Miniaturized/portable analytical systems/devices have attracted great attention because of its easy-operation, portability and on-site analysis.

Presently, a number of portable analytical methods/devices were developed for the detection of NO2 -. Lu et al. using smartphone as detector developed a novel paper-based microfluidic colorimetric device for the detection of trace NO2 - in pork based on Griess reaction [25]. Mesquita et al. developed two novel microfluidic paper-based analytical devices for the quantification of NO2 - and NO3 - in human saliva samples based on the Griess method [26]. Benito-Lopez et al. reported a handheld miniaturized polymer platform with a paper-based microfluidic device for simultaneous detection of NO2 - and NO3 - in fresh and seawater samples [27].

In sealed containers, gas-generation reaction can lead to substantial pressure increases, which can be readily read out by a portable gas pressure meter [28]. Yang’s group initially introduced a portable gas pressure meter into analytical chemistry for highly sensitive bioanalysis, opening up a new way for sensitive biomedical analysis [29]. The portable gas pressure meter was subsequently applied to the analysis of various targets, such as ions [30], microRNA [31,32] and protein markers [33,34] etc. The gas pressure meter-based analytical system is an ideal strategy for on-site and rapid quantitative analysis for its inherent advantages of portability, signal amplification and easy-used property. Fortunately, as far as we known, the use of portable gas pressure meter for NO2 - detection has not been reported yet.

In this work, a portable, rapid and easy-used analytical method was established for the detection of NO2 - by means of a portable gas pressure meter. Specific reaction between NO2 - and sodium cyclamate was occurred, gases of cyclohexene and nitrogen were formed in the reaction bottle. The gas pressure in reaction bottle was measured by portable gas pressure meter, and the analysis of NO2 - was thus achieved. The interference from the sample matrix could be eliminated efficiently thanks to the specific reaction of this method. No analytical instruments and professional operators were required, only a portable gas pressure meter was used. This portable gas pressure meter-based method was further used for NO2 - analysis in several water and ham sausage samples. It is a potential portable, rapid and easy-used method for NO2 - on-site analysis.

Reagents used here were at least analytical grade. 18.2 MΩ cm deionized water (DIW) was used throughout in whole work. DIW was produced from a water purification system (Chengdu Ultrapure Technology Co., Ltd, Chengdu, China). Sodium cyclamate, sulfanilamide, N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NED) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NaNO2, NaNO3, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, NaF, NaBr, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, BaCl2, MgCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Na3PO4·12H2O, Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, Na2B4O7·10H2O, K4[Fe(CN)6]·3H2O, H2SO4, HCl, acetic acid and activated charcoal were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Tap water (W1) was collected from our campus. River water (W4) and lake water (W7) were collected from Dongfengqu River and Qinglong Lake (Chengdu, China), respectively. Three ham sausage samples (H1, H4 and H7) were purchased from local market.

Gas pressure meter was purchased from PASSTECH (Xiamen, China) and used as a detector in this work. An ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (T2602 dual beam UV-Vis, Shanghai Yoke Instrument Co., Ltd, China) was used for the analysis of NO2 - (Griess method).

According to the requirements of the China National Food Safety Standard (GB 5009.33-2016) [10], the extraction and separation of NO2 - in ham sausage samples were processed with some modifications. The sample preparation was as follow: 5 g smashed ham sausage sample was added in beaker, 10 mL 50 g L−1 saturated Na2B4O7 solution and 20 mL hot water (70 ℃) were added later, well-mixed. It was heated in a 75 ℃ water bath for 10 min, and then cooled to room temperature. For precipitating the proteins, 5 mL 106 g L−1 of K4[Fe(CN)6] solution and 5 mL 220 g L−1 of Zn(CH3COO)2 solution were added in sequence and shaken well. This mixture was diluted to 50 mL with DIW and mixed well. Next, the mixture was left for 30 min and then filtered by filter paper, the initial filtrate was thus obtained. Finally, 2 g activated charcoal was added to the obtained filtrate, mixed and reacted for 5 min, and then filtered with filter paper. The resulting filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before subsequent testing.
Three water samples (tap water (W1), river water (W4) and lake water (W7)) were filtrated by 0.22 µm filter membrane before analysis, and then detected by gas pressure meter.

According to the regulations of the China National Food Safety Standard (GB 5009.33-2016), Griess colorimetry is one of the standard method for the determination of nitrite (NO2 -). As a consequence, the analytical results of NO2 - in this work were also verified by Griess method.
(1)
Preparation of sulfanilamide solution (4 g L
−1
): 0.4 g sulfanilamide was dissolved in 100 mL 20 % (v/v) hydrochloric acid (HCl), mixed evenly, and stored in a brown bottle in dark before used.
(2)
Preparation of N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NED) (2 g L
−1
): 0.2 g of NED was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, well-mixed and stored in dark.
(3)
Preparation of standard solution/sample solution: 0.2 mL of NO2 - standard solution or sample solution was added into a headspace bottle. Then, 0.1 mL 4 g L−1 sulfanilamide solution was added, mixed thoroughly, and allowed it to stand for 5 min. And then, 0.1 mL 2 g L−1 NED and 4.6 mL deionized water were added subsequently. After 20 min, the solution was measured by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-Vis) at a wavelength of 540 nm.

The portable analytical system was consisted of a portable gas pressure meter and several headspace bottles. As shown in Scheme 1, analytical procedures can be summarized as below. Step I (Sample): 6 mL NO2 - standard solution or sample solution was added into the headspace bottle, 0.2 mL sodium cyclamate solution (10 g L−1) and 0.1 mL H2SO4 solution (15 mmol L−1) were added subsequently. Step II (Reaction): after all solutions have been added, the reaction bottle was sealed. This headspace bottle was heated in a water bath at 70 °C for 30 min (Attention: 70 °C was reaction temperature, not the detection temperature, all samples were measured at room temperature). Step III (Detection): as above-mentioned, in sealed reaction bottles, gas-generation reaction can lead significant pressure changes, which can be detected by a gas pressure meter [28]. Gases of cyclohexene and N2 were generated during Step II (Reaction) procedure, because of the specific reaction between NO2 - and sodium cyclamate (reaction 1), the interferences from the matrix can be eliminated efficiently. The proposed method exhibits a good selectivity for NO2 - detection. After the sealed headspace bottles have cooled to room temperature (Attention: all samples were measured at room temperature), the gas pressures in the bottles were measured by a gas pressure meter at room temperature, the detection time for every sample was within 30 s.

The schematic diagram of NO2 - detection by portable gas pressure meter was shown in Scheme 1. As previous reported [35–37], NO2 - can specific reacted with sodium cyclamate under acidic condition, cyclohexene and nitrogen (N2) were generated after reaction (reaction 1), and the gaseous components of reaction 1 were identified by gas chromatography mass spectrometry (GC-MS) in previous work [35], so that it was not discussed in this work. The pressure changes caused by gas formation (reaction 1, cyclohexene and N2) can be detected by a portable gas pressure meter. With the increase of NO2 - concentration, the production of cyclohexene and nitrogen (N2) gases also increased, resulting in a corresponding increase of pressures in the sealed reaction bottles, the concentration of NO2 - were proportional to the change of pressures in bottles, the detection of NO2 - was thus achieved.
(1)
Image 1

As abovementioned, the proposed analytical system was based on the specific reaction between NO2 - and sodium cyclamate, so that the feasibility of this gas generation reaction for NO2 - detection was discussed. The pressure responses were monitored by gas pressure meter under different conditions: (1) NaNO2; (2) NaNO2 + sodium cyclamate; (3) NaNO2 + H2SO4; (4) NaNO2 + sodium cyclamate + H2SO4. All of those conditions were treated under 70 °C (reaction temperature) water bath for 30 min and detected at room temperature. As presented in Fig. 1, no obvious pressure signals were observed for (1) NaNO2, (2) NaNO2 + sodium cyclamate and (3) NaNO2 + H2SO4, demonstrating no gases were generated under those conditions, they have no effect on pressure detection. As expected, only when all of the reagents ((4) NaNO2 + sodium cyclamate + H2SO4) were added, a noticeable pressure change (15.1 kPa) was observed, indicating the feasibility of gas pressure meter for NO2 - quantitative detection.

To obtain better experimental results, the relevant experimental conditions were optimized, including sodium cyclamate concentration, H2SO4 concentration, reaction temperature and reaction time. The analysis of NO2 - in this work was based on the change of gas pressure (reaction 1), so that sodium cyclamate played a crucial role in this work. As shown in Fig. 2a, the concentration of sodium cyclamate was discussed here. With the increase of sodium cyclamate, the gas pressure values were increased gradually, and then reached a plateau after 10 g L−1. So that 10 g L−1 sodium cyclamate was used as the optimal condition in further experiments. According to the previous report [36], H2SO4 was required and served as a catalyst for reaction 1, no cyclohexene was formed from the nitrite-sodium cyclamate system without H2SO4. Therefore, the effect of H2SO4 was also evaluated (Fig. 2b). The gas pressure value was increased with the increase of H2SO4 concentration and then reached a plateau. 15 mmol L−1 H2SO4 was selected and used for subsequent experiments. Reaction temperature and reaction time also have an influence on reaction yield and they were also discussed here (Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d). With the increase of reaction temperature and reaction time, the gas pressure value was changed correspondingly, and the optimal conditions for reaction temperature and reaction time were observed at 70 ℃ and 30 min, respectively.
The optimized experimental conditions can be summarized as: concentration of sodium cyclamate: 10 g L−1; concentration of H2SO4: 15 mmol L−1; reaction temperature: 70 ℃ and reaction time: 30 min.

The stability of the portable gas pressure meter was evaluated ( Fig. 3). NO2 - solution was added in 20 separated bottles and reacted with sodium cyclamate under above-mentioned conditions. After cooling to room temperature, the pressure was measured by the gas pressure meter. The average gas pressure value of the 20 separated bottles was 7.3 kPa and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.4 % was thus obtained, exhibiting a good stability of this method.

The analysis principle of this method was based on the specific reaction between NO2 - and sodium cyclamate (reaction 1), and then the pressure was measured by gas pressure meter, so that the interferences from the matrix could be eliminated efficiently, exhibiting a good ability of anti-interference. To discuss the interferences from co-existing ions in actual samples for the detection of NO2 -, 11 cations and 7 anions were discussed, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+and Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, F-, Br-, PO4 3-, CO3 2-. It can be seen from the Fig. 4, the interferences from those co-existing ions could be neglected, although their concentration were 10-fold (500 mg L−1) than NO2 -, demonstrating a good anti-interference ability.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the analytical performance of this work was evaluated by a portable gas pressure meter. As shown in Fig. 5a, with the increased of NO2 - concentration, the gas pressure value was increased correspondingly. The gas pressure values showed a good linear relationship with NO2 -in the range of 2–100 mg L−1 (43–2174 μmol L−1), and the linear correlation coefficient was better than 0.99. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.65 mg L−1 (14 μmol L−1), defined as 3 times the standard deviation of the signal (blank, n = 11) divided by the slope of the calibration curve. To assess the analytical performance of this work, Table 1 summarized the analytical figures of merit of this work and compared with other reported works, demonstrating a low LOD, wide linear range and good applicability of this method.

For further verifying the applicability and feasibility of this system, it was used for NO2 - detection in water samples and various ham sausage samples. The analytical results of those samples and their spiked samples were presented in Table 2. The content of NO2 - in water samples (tap water, Dongfengqu River water and Qinglong Lake water) were analyzed by gas pressure meter, NO2 - was not detected. For further demonstrating the analytical results, the spiked samples of those water samples were also analyzed, with recoveries of 98–106 %. Furthermore, the application of this system for food analysis was also verified by the analysis of three ham sausage samples and their spiked samples, recoveries ranging 94 %–104 %. In addition, Griess method was used for further confirming the accuracy of those analytical results (Fig. 5b and Table 2). The analytical results of this method were matched well with the Griess method, indicating a good accuracy and reliability of the portable gas pressure meter.

A simple, rapid and easy-used gas pressure meter-based analytical system was developed for NO2 - detection in water samples and ham sausage samples. The specific reaction between NO2 - and sodium cyclamate was occurred under acidic condition, gases (cyclohexene and N2) were generated and inducing the gas pressures of reaction bottles changed, gas pressure meter was used as a miniaturized detector for NO2 - detection. A linear range of 2–100 mg L−1 (43–2174 μmol L−1) was obtained and the developed portable analytical system was further applied to the determination of NO2 - in various samples (water samples and ham sausage samples) and their spiked samples, with recoveries of 94–106 %. This analytical device/system is a promising miniaturized/portable method for real-time and on-site analysis of NO2 - in food samples and environmental water samples.

Jing Xiao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing-original draft. Jiayuan Tang: Investigation, Validation. Jihong Chen: Investigation, Validation. Lanlan Li: Investigation, Validation. Shu Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing-review & editing. Xiaoli Xiong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-review & editing. Zhirong Zou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22106113) and Sichuan Science & Technology Program (No. 2023NSFSC1112).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jing Xiao is a master student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward miniaturized/portable pressure meter-based analytical devices/systems.

Jiayuan Tang is a master student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward miniaturized/portable pressure meter-based analytical devices/systems.
Jihong Chen is a master student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward miniaturized/portable analytical devices/systems.
Lanlan Li is a master student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward miniaturized/portable analytical devices/systems.
Shu Zhang is a professor of College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. Her current research interest is analytical spectroscopy.

Xiaoli Xiong received her BSc degree in chemical engineering in 1994 from Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, MSc in 2005 in analytical chemistry from Sichuan University and was an associate professor since 2007 at Southwest University of Science and Technology. She moved to Sichuan Normal University as associate professor of analytical chemistry in 2011. Her current research interests are analytical chemistry, biosensors, chemosensors and nano-materials.

Zhirong Zou received his BSc degree in 2013 from College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Ph.D degree in 2018 in analytical chemistry from Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University. He began his own independent career in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. His current research interest is focus on chemical vapor generation-based miniaturized/portable analytical systems/devices.
